title: OnePlus, the ‘Flagship-Killer’ Smartphone Brand, Is All but Dead
gist: OnePlus, an Oppo/BBK Electronics subbrand, is effectively winding down outside China: WIRED confirmed layoffs and North America/Europe staff transitions to Oppo/Realme, its US shipments collapsed roughly 90% from 1 million in 2019 to under 130,000 in 2025, and its sales mix flipped from 22% US in 2021 to 56% China by 2025, amid a broader global smartphone shipment decline (-11% YoY in Q2 2026) driven by an AI-data-center-fueled RAM shortage that only Apple and Samsung are weathering.

insights.claim: OnePlus, a subsidiary of Oppo (the fourth-largest global smartphone maker), has been laying off staff across regions over recent months, with many European employees transitioning to roles at Oppo or Realme; WIRED verified this by speaking with former employees and tracking LinkedIn updates, with most departures occurring between March and June 2026.
insights.speaker: Julian Chokkattu
insights.so_what: Directly documented layoffs and staff reassignment confirm OnePlus is being wound down as a standalone brand outside China, a concrete signal for anyone tracking BBK Electronics' portfolio strategy or supply-chain exposure to OnePlus.
insights.locator: The company, a subsidiary of Oppo

insights.claim: Oppo's official statement to WIRED said Realme will focus on overseas markets and stop launching new products in China, while OnePlus's China product roadmap 'remains unchanged' — a statement that pointedly avoids confirming OnePlus's status in North America and Europe.
insights.speaker: Julian Chokkattu
insights.so_what: Oppo's selective, evasive statement is itself a signal: the company is reallocating its sub-brand portfolio by geography, effectively consolidating OnePlus into a China-only brand while staying silent on its Western wind-down.
insights.quote: To further consolidate resources and enhance global product strategy synergy, Realme will focus on overseas markets and no longer launch new products in China. OnePlus' product roadmap in China remains unchanged.

insights.claim: An anonymous former employee laid off in April 2026, whose entire New York City office was cut, describes the decision as made unilaterally 'from the top' with no input channels for the team.
insights.speaker: Julian Chokkattu
insights.so_what: First-hand account of the New York office's full closure corroborates that OnePlus's North American operations, not just marginal roles, are being eliminated.
insights.quote: This was a kind of 'from the top' decision with no input channels for anyone on the team.
insights.locator: A source, who asked to remain anonymous

insights.claim: Rumors reported by The Verge indicate Oppo will transition existing OnePlus devices from OxygenOS to Oppo's ColorOS software in the coming months, though device owners can roll back to OxygenOS while likely losing future updates.
insights.speaker: Julian Chokkattu
insights.so_what: A forced software-platform migration for existing OnePlus device owners is a concrete end-of-brand-identity signal and a customer-retention/support risk that could accelerate churn to competitors.
insights.locator: Update: The company tells The Verge

themes.theme: OnePlus's collapse in US market share

insights.claim: OnePlus's US smartphone shipments fell from roughly 1 million units in 2019 to just under 130,000 in 2025 — a roughly 90 percent volume drop over six years, according to IDC senior research director Nabila Popal.
insights.speaker: Julian Chokkattu
insights.so_what: A precise, sourced 90% six-year volume collapse in the US quantifies just how thoroughly OnePlus lost its Western foothold, useful as a cautionary benchmark for other challenger smartphone brands attempting to break into the carrier-dominated US market.
insights.locator: OnePlus went from 1 million smartphone shipments

insights.claim: OnePlus's sales mix shifted dramatically by region: the US represented about 22% of shipments in 2021 (similar for Europe) with only 18% from China, but by 2025 the numbers flipped to 56% from China, and Asia Pacific overall rose to 91% of volume from 51% in 2021, per Nabila Popal of IDC.
insights.speaker: Julian Chokkattu
insights.so_what: The geographic sales-mix inversion (from Western-weighted to China/Asia-Pacific-weighted) explains and precedes Oppo's decision to preserve only the China roadmap — a clear leading indicator investors could have used to anticipate the Western wind-down.

insights.claim: OnePlus's US market share fell from 1.8 percent in 2021 to 0.1 percent in 2025, while Apple and Samsung's combined US market share rose from 73 percent to 80 percent over the same period, according to IDC's Nabila Popal.
insights.speaker: Julian Chokkattu
insights.so_what: Quantifies accelerating US smartphone-market consolidation around Apple and Samsung, a structural headwind for any non-carrier-aligned challenger brand (Chinese or otherwise) attempting entry.

insights.claim: OnePlus's US sales took a sharp downturn after T-Mobile dropped its carrier partnership with the brand in 2023; IDC's Nabila Popal notes carriers drive as much as 66 percent of US smartphone volume, based on 2025 data, making carrier access essentially required for US market success.
insights.speaker: Julian Chokkattu
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific causal trigger (loss of T-Mobile carrier distribution) for OnePlus's US collapse and quantifies carrier gatekeeping as the structural barrier to entry in the US smartphone market — a key risk factor for any phone brand not carrier-aligned.
insights.locator: Still, the company's sales took a nosedive

insights.claim: IDC's Nabila Popal explains OnePlus's pricing strategy failure: after launching the OnePlus 6 in 2018 at $529 as a proudly positioned 'flagship killer,' the company raised prices toward premium-market levels rather than holding its value position, making it too similar to competitors it could no longer underprice, a strategy she says works only for Apple and Samsung.
insights.speaker: Julian Chokkattu
insights.so_what: A clear pricing-strategy framework — 'grab share cheap, then raise price' — that failed for OnePlus but works only for the two dominant brands; a transferable lesson for evaluating any challenger consumer-hardware brand's premiumization strategy.
insights.quote: In 2018, with the OnePlus 6, they had launched what they were calling very proudly a 'flagship killer' at the price of $529, with flagship specs... And then, rather than staying at that price point, they replicated the premium market—trying to increase prices—and that made them similar to the competition... unfortunately, certain brands are not able to command a price beyond a certain point. It's really only been Apple and Samsung that have been able to operate very successfully.
insights.locator: Popal says it's a strategy often set by companies

themes.theme: Global smartphone market: AI-driven memory shortage

insights.claim: Research firm Counterpoint reported an 11 percent year-over-year decline in global smartphone shipments in Q2 2026, the lowest Q2 shipment level in 13 years, driven by a global RAM shortage caused by memory chips being consumed by AI data centers; Apple and Samsung grew while Xiaomi, Oppo, and Vivo saw the sharpest declines.
insights.speaker: Julian Chokkattu
insights.so_what: A direct, quantified causal link between AI data-center memory demand and a 13-year-low smartphone shipment decline is a significant cross-sector data point: AI infrastructure buildout is now materially constraining consumer electronics supply chains, benefiting scale players (Apple, Samsung) over smaller Chinese OEMs.
insights.locator: But OnePlus' exit comes at a time when smartphone shipments

insights.claim: OnePlus raised the price of its smartwatch from $330 to $500 last year at the onset of President Trump's tariff war, and hiked prices on its latest phones in India in May 2026, indicating tariff and cost pressures compounding its existing market-share erosion.
insights.speaker: Julian Chokkattu
insights.so_what: Tariff-driven price hikes on top of an already-collapsing US market position illustrate how trade policy can accelerate the exit of marginal foreign consumer-electronics brands from the US market.
insights.locator: Last year, at the onset of president Donald Trump's tariff war

insights.claim: OnePlus joins a growing list of smartphone brands that have shut down, exited the mobile business, pivoted, or scaled back ambitions, including HTC, LG Mobile, Sony, Meizu, and HMD, reflecting IDC's Nabila Popal's view that the market is consolidating further around fewer brands.
insights.speaker: Julian Chokkattu
insights.so_what: Frames OnePlus's decline as part of a longer-running structural consolidation trend in global smartphones, useful context for assessing remaining mid-tier brand viability (e.g., Nothing, Motorola, Google Pixel) going forward.
insights.quote: It's a pity that US consumers do not have the choice of the number of brands that are available globally because there are some fascinating technologies and features. But I don't see that landscape changing.
insights.locator: OnePlus now joins a growing list of companies
